coredata.dc:title: Rational design for the microplasma synthesis from vitamin B9 to N-doped carbon quantum dots towards selected applications
coredata.dc:creator: Pho, Quoc Hue
Lin, LiangLiang
Tran, Nam Nghiep
Tran, Tung T.
Nguyen, An Hoa
Losic, Dusan
Rebrov, Evgeny V.
Hessel, Volker
coredata.dc:description: N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQD) are rationally designed and synthesised, for the first time, from folic acid (Vitamin B9) by a non-thermal microplasma jet. A new conceptual design was developed to synthesise the desirable NCQD for three main applications (nanopesticides, water purification, and theranostic treatment). The structural and analytical characterisation confirmed an average size of 3.1 nm for the synthesised NCQD with the multi-functional groups (-OH, –COOH, –NH2) on their surface. The TEM results indicated that the core of NCQD was a multilayered structure, including single defected graphene sheets of graphitic-nitrogen and pyrrolic-nitrogen. In addition, fluorescence performance and stability of the as-prepared NCQD were determined. The quantum yield of NCQD was 35%, which is relatively high, with a strong blue fluorescence. A basis for predicting colloidal behaviours based on balancing molecular attractive and repulsive forces was elucidated by applying the Derjaguin, Landau, Vervey, and Overbeek (DLVO) theory. Finally, compared with other similar microplasma-assisted synthesis processes, this developed method has proven the ability to provide a tailored and scalable synthesis process of high-quantum-yield NCQD at gram-scale production.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQD) are rationally designed and synthesised, for the first time, from folic acid (Vitamin B9) by a non-thermal microplasma jet. A new conceptual design was developed to synthesise the desirable NCQD for three main applications (nanopesticides, water purification, and theranostic treatment). The structural and analytical characterisation confirmed an average size of 3.1 nm for the synthesised NCQD with the multi-functional groups (-OH, –COOH, –NH2) on their surface. The TEM results indicated that the core of NCQD was a multilayered structure, including single defected graphene sheets of graphitic-nitrogen and pyrrolic-nitrogen. In addition, fluorescence performance and stability of the as-prepared NCQD were determined. The quantum yield of NCQD was 35%, which is relatively high, with a strong blue fluorescence. A basis for predicting colloidal behaviours based on balancing molecular attractive and repulsive forces was elucidated by applying the Derjaguin, Landau, Vervey, and Overbeek (DLVO) theory. Finally, compared with other similar microplasma-assisted synthesis processes, this developed method has proven the ability to provide a tailored and scalable synthesis process of high-quantum-yield NCQD at gram-scale production.

ce:section.ce:para: After the first discovery in 2004, carbon dots (CD) have attracted considerable attention in exploring various potential applications because of their outstanding properties, including their low toxicity [1], biocompatibility [2], water-solubility [3] and excellent optical properties [4].

A broad range of starting materials as carbon sources, from organic compounds (ethylenimine [5], citric acid [6], urea [7], chitosan [8], sucrose [9], cystine [10], etc.) and biomass materials (orange peels [11], banana peels [12], bamboo leave [13], prawn shells [14], etc.), has been explored over the last decade for CD synthesis [15]. As a result, an extensive library of CD with diverse structures and properties has been created using various synthetic techniques, including laser ablation [16], arc discharge [17], hydrothermal [18], pyrolysis [19], solvothermal [20], ultrasonic [21], etc. Most of these CD synthesis strategies can be classified as the trial-and-error approach in searching for suitable applications [22]. It is a time-consuming journey with cost- and process inefficiency to create CD with suitable structure and properties available for a specific application.

Instead, another alternative approach to producing a high-quality CD for a target application is to focus on “design-for purpose” before carrying out the synthesis processes, which is called “a rational design approach” [23]. In principle, the first step of this approach is to scientifically define the problems that need to be solved using CD with specific properties.
Based on the precise problem definition, a conceptual design of CD solution can be proposed in response to the defined problems for the scientific check.

It can be repeated many times and improved until the rationally designed nanomaterials meet the requirements to solve the defined problems. Therefore, defining problems in applications will give us information on which properties of CD need to be created and adjusted to establish final strategic synthetic pathways.

With the aforementioned evidence in Table 1, we proposed a conceptual design of NCQD guided by three molecular targets (Fig. 1): (1) surface functional group control (characterised by HNMR, FTIR, XPS, UV–Vis and fluorescein isothiocyanate); (2) crystallographic control (characterised by XRD and Raman spectroscopy); (3) bulk/size control (characterised by TEM and HR-TEM). In addition, gross-product property targets concerning fluorescence and stability were determined.

After the conceptual design of N-doped carbon quantum dots, a suitable synthesis strategy is required to ensure that the rationally designed NCQD is obtained in higher quantity and quality. In this rational design concept, bottom-up synthesis approaches are more dominant than these top-down counterparts to meet the requirements of the proposed conceptual design [31]. First, it is more flexible by using various starting materials with different functional groups [32]. Second, these approaches do not undergo further surface passivation, and heteroatoms are easily introduced into NCQD [33]. For this reason, we aim to follow the bottom-up synthesis approach to produce NCQD.

Yet, the current challenges of conventional bottom-up synthesis processes are the high temperature [43], high pressure [44], and strong acids [45], which lead to energy inefficiency, multiple processes, and unavoidable harm to the environment. To avoid this problem, we use the kINPen®IND plasma jet for the first time as a new synthesis method of bottom-up approaches to design NCQD rationally. However, the critical issue in microplasma synthesis of NCQD is low mass-production yield and productivity [46]. In this case, process miniaturisation might help conduct a more controlled process to enhance productivity [47]. Thus, the second goal of this paper is to use kINPen®IND plasma to produce rationally designed NCQD using folic acid as a green and non-toxic source of nitrogen and carbon in NCQD synthesis [48].

After the synthesis of rationally designed NCQD is implemented, the performance of this material will be tested with photoluminescence. Technically, the safety of NCQD in applications is related to their stability. This study has finished at the safety stage, with DLVO theory being used to understand more of their colloidal behaviours. For this reason, economics and circularity research has not been done yet at this stage. Nonetheless, our previous study reported using green chemistry and circular and EcoScale metrics to evaluate various synthesis approaches to NCQD fabrication. As a result, this study has pointed out that the microplasma-assisted synthesis approach might have much higher sustainable potential than its counterparts. Therefore, our current study on using the kINPen®IND microplasma jet to synthesise NCQD is conducted to explore that potential [47].

Sodium hydrogen carbonate, folic acid, hydrochloric acid, quinine sulphate, sulfuric acid (98%), and fluorescein 5(6)-isothiocyanate were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, which were of analytical grades and used without further purification. kINPen®IND microplasma jet was purchased from Neoplas GmbH, Germany.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental setup for the synthesis of NCQD
ce:section.ce:para: NCQD were synthesised in an open reactor with a 9 mm × 15 mm (W x H) dimension under kINPen®IND-induced microplasma. The microplasma jet with a dimension of 105 x 180 × 330 mm (H x W x D) was connected with a 110 VAC power supply (50 Hz). A continuous Ar (99.9%) gas stream with a flow rate of 2 sccm was injected into the jet at the pressure of 3 bar to form microplasma. An optical fibre was inserted into the plasma-liquid interaction region to detect reactive species formed from microplasma. The length of the formed microplasma was about 1 cm. The synthesis procedure was described in Fig. 2 . Briefly, folic acid (FA) solution as a starting carbon source was prepared by dissolving in NaHCO3 (0.5 M) as a solvent under stirring until the resulting orange solution was homogeneously mixed. The FA solution was then transferred to the open reactor with 0.5 g of metal flakes (500–1000 μm) to intensity the electrical field with plasma-catalytic effects for enhanced photoluminescence of NCDQ. Afterwards, the microplasma formed by the kINPen®IND jet was applied to treat the solution for a given time. After completing the treatment, the resulting solution turned dark brown. To collect NCQD, the treated solution was transferred to a beaker and precipitated by HCl 0.5 M. After precipitation, the mixture was centrifugated (10,000 rpm) for 30 min. The un-reacted FA pellet was removed, while the supernatant liquid was then dialysed against water in a dialysis bag (1000 Da) for three days. Finally, pure NCQD were obtained in power by a freeze dryer.

The transmission electron microscopy measurements on FEI Titan Themis 80-200 operated at 200 kV is a powerful technique with high resolution, which was used to investigate the structure, particle size and distribution, and morphology of the rationally designed NCQD. Briefly, 10 mg of NCQD were dispersed into water under sonication until NCQD became homogeneously. Later on, the NCQD solution was dropped into a TEM grid. Then, the resulting TEM grid was dried entirely in a convection oven at 60 °C to remove solvents. Finally, the grid was measured with FEI Titan Themis 80-200 to analyse the as-prepared NCQD material.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) from SPECS Sage XPS was a technique for analysing the surface chemistry of NCQD. The preparation of XPS samples was similar to TEM preparation (10 mg/ml). Later on, the NCQD solution was drop-casted into a silicon wafer. Then, the resulting silicon wafer was dried up in the oven at 50 °C to remove water. Finally, the silicon wafer was investigated by SPECS Sage XPS to record data.

ce:section.ce:section-title: X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) and Raman spectroscopy
ce:section.ce:para: The degree of crystallinity structure of NCQD was measured/characterised by X-ray Powder Diffraction (XRD)-Rigaku MiniFlex 600. Cu-Kα1 radiation with a wavelength of 1.54056 Å was used in the diffractometer. The samples were scanned at a speed of 0.2°/min from 10 to 90°.

Raman spectroscopy-HORIBA LabRAM HR Evolution is a chemical analysis device that does not destroy the components of samples, which was used to investigate NCQD's chemical structure and crystallinity. The measurement was carried out using the 512 nm-wavelength laser as the excitation.

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR): The Nicolet 6700 FTIR spectrometer was employed to obtain an infrared spectrum of absorption of NCQD. From the spectrum, functional groups on the surface of NCQD were characterised.

Proton nuclear magnetic resonance (1HNMR): The Agilent 600 MHz NMR spectrometer was used for molecular structure characterisation of NCQD. 20 mg of the sample was dissolved into DMSO‑d 6 solvent overnight to ensure that all samples were well-dissolved. Finally, the samples were inserted into The Agilent 600 MHz NMR spectrometer for recording data.

The fluorescence spectrum was carried out on a photoluminescence Spectrometer (FLS 1000, United Kingdom). Firstly, photoluminescence 2D colour mapping was investigated to determine the as-prepared NCQD's maximal excitation and emission wavelength. The excitation and emission spectra were set from 300 to 700 nm and plotted in photoluminescence 2D colour mapping. After investigating the excitation and emission wavelength, these parameter values were used for further photoluminescence intensity tests. 1 mL of the as-prepared NCQD (1 μg/mL) was inserted into the FLS1000 under the investigated excitation light source to determine the photoluminescence curve at the maximal wavelength.

UV–Vis spectra are another important optical parameter that needs to investigate. Briefly, 1 mL of the as-prepared NCQD (1 μg/mL) was inserted into Cary 60 UV–Vis Agilent Technologies to record the spectra to determine the maximal absorbance wavelength. Based on this data, further absorbance tests are carried out.

Fluorescein isothiocyanate (FTIC) assay has been widely used to attach amine groups. The isothiocyanate groups in FTIC can react with primary amines to form isothiourea conjugate. To determine –NH2 functional groups on the surface of NCQD, fluorescein-5-isothiocyanate (1 μg/mL) was used to react with NCQD (1 μg/mL). The resulting mixture was tested with photoluminescence spectroscopy (FSL1000).

The quantum yield of fluorescent NCQD was measured based on UV–Vis and photoluminescence spectroscopy. Quinine sulphate in 0.1 M H2SO4 aqueous solution has a similar intense blue fluorescence of carbon dots. Thus, it was used as a standard fluorescent agent with a fluorescence quantum yield of 0.54. The quantum yield of NCQD was calculated as following equation (1):

ce:section.ce:section-title: The colloidal behaviours in aqueous solutions by using Derjaguin, Landau, Vervey, and Overbeek (DLVO) theory
ce:section.ce:para:
In agricultural applications, the colloidal behaviour of the as-prepared NCQD in water plays a vital role in hydrophilic nanopesticides. For this reason, Derjaguin, Landau, Vervey, and Overbeek (DLVO) theory was studied in this research . In principle, the DLVO theory is the total effect of van der Waals interactions (V H ) and electrical double layer interactions (VD), as shown in equations (2)–(4):
(2) VT = VH + VD

where r (nm) is the radius of NCQD; h (nm) is the distance from the surface to surface between two NCQD; A12 is the Hamaker constant, 6.26 × 10− 21 J; kT is the Boltzmann temperature, e0 is the permittivity of free space; e is electron charge; kB is Boltzmann constant;
ε

is zeta potential (mV); k−1 is the Debye length.

Three key influential parameters on the behaviour of NCQD in water include particle size, temperature, and potential with surface charges. Table 2 showed different scenarios for studying colloidal behaviours of NCQD in water. In this study, the mean particle size was calculated as 3.13 ± 0.65 nm, and the environment temperature was 298.15 (0K). Therefore, the only influential parameter on their behaviours is the zeta potential with charge, as shown in Table 2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of pH and water solubility on photoluminescence of NCQD
ce:section.ce:para: Various NCQD solutions were prepared with pH values (2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 14) and various solvents (ethanol, acetone, acetonitrile, and water) for dissolving NCQD. All the solution samples were characterised by photoluminescence spectroscopy (FLS1000).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rational design strategy for the synthesis of NCQD
ce:section.ce:para: A portable atmospheric-pressure plasma device, known as kINPen®IND, was explored here to synthesise N-CQDs, which has not been explored before [49]. For the first time, our study has demonstrated its potential in fabricating NCQD. This study selected various starting materials for the synthesis process, including urea, vitamin C, and folic acid. The strategy for this synthesis was the miniaturisation process, where a reactor with a small volume was designed to maximise plasma-liquid interaction. Thus, various reactors were used to optimise the synthesis process based on plasma-liquid interaction, consisting of the Petri dish, Eppendorf, glass beaker, and our reactors.

The initial results in Table 3 showed that citric acid/urea and vitamin C could not produce NCQD under microplasma, though these precursors are available for other synthesis methods [ [50,51]]. In contrast, folic acid shows good performance when exposed to microplasma [48]. In addition, we also tested folic acid under various types of reactors. The results revealed that our container with an optimal dimension results in the best performance of the synthesis process. Thus, this reactor was used for fabricating NCQD. The mass reaction efficiency of the described-above reaction conditions was 17%, which is very high for a nanomaterial product. Those or related efficiencies, being usually much lower, are rarely mentioned in nanomaterials literature. This research achieves process intensification, bringing the synthesis to 1 g per day. The formation of NCQD was confirmed by optical emission spectroscopy (OES) in Fig. 3 where various reactive species were detected. The OES results indicates that folic acid was decomposed by microplasma jet to form various reactive species such as OHꞏ, CHꞏ, ꞉CC꞉, ꞏCN, and NHꞏ. Reactive species like CHꞏ and ꞉CC꞉ are considered as intermediates which may conjugates each other to form graphite-like structure. In contrast, ꞏCN and NHꞏ can react with CHꞏ and ꞉CC꞉ to form nitrogen defects (graphitic and pyrrolic N) in graphite-like structure. Finally, OHꞏ and NHꞏ can conjugate with graphite-like structure on the surface to form –OH and –NH2 functional groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Size and morphology of the as-prepared NCQD
ce:section.ce:para: The TEM image (Fig. 4 A) showed spherical NCQD with a particle size of 3.13 ± 0.65 nm. In addition, the synthesised NCQD also showed a narrow size distribution ranging from 1 to 10 nm, indicating a well-distributed particle size. Based on the definition and classification from Valcárcel et al. (2016) [25], the as-prepared carbon dots in our study are classified as carbon quantum dots with multilayered graphene sheets. Table S1 compared TEM images from various types of carbon dots in terms of structure and morphology. More specifically, the HR-TEM pattern (Fig. 4B) showed spherical quantum dots with a lattice spacing of 0.27 nm, indicating the high crystallinity of NCQD with graphite structure. The interlayer spacing of NCQD was about 0.27 nm, which corresponds to the (002) plane of the graphite structure. The lattice spacing of 0.27 nm (calculated by performing Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) from Image J software) means the spacing between two graphene layers [52]. In addition, the XRD and Raman spectroscopy results in our study also revealed that the as-prepared NCQD are highly crystalline (see section 3.3.1).

The Raman spectrum in Fig. 5 A showed two distinctive peaks at 1338 cm−1 and 1558 cm−1, attributed to the D and G bands [46]. This result indicates that NCQD has a crystalline structure. The G peak results from stretching vibrations of sp2 carbon atoms in both rings and chains, whereas the D peak represents some structural defects in graphene structure due to the breathing modes of sp2 atoms in rings. A D band peak at 1338 cm−1 indicated that the addition of nitrogen elements could contribute to the defect in the graphitic structure of carbon dots. The ratio of the intensity of D/G peaks is an important parameter, revealing the defects in the structure of NCQD. D/G ratio is relevant to the ratio of the number of sp3 to sp2 carbon atoms. Based on the Raman spectroscopy, the value of ID/IG was 0.26, implying that more sp2-hybridized carbons were formed in a two-dimension hexagonal lattice. Apart from two distinctive peaks attributed to the D and G bands, another two peaks were observed at 1129 cm−1 and 1234 cm−1. These two typical peaks are attributed to trans-polyacetylene (trans-PA), which is an alternate chain of sp2 carbon atoms, with single hydrogen bonded to each carbon [53].

In agreement with the Raman spectrum, the XRD pattern of NCQD (Fig. 5B) showed a broad peak at 25.5°. The results indicate that the crystalline structure of folic acid has been decomposed by microplasma to form crystalline carbon dots. The broad peak at 25.5° corresponds to the (002) surface. This result reveals that the interlayer spacing (d) of the NCQD (0.27 nm) was lower than that of the interlayer spacing of graphene structure (0.33 nm). Thus, in agreement with HR-TEM, XRD patterns and Raman spectroscopy confirm carbon dots’ high crystalline nature, attributing to the generation of oxygen and nitrogen-containing groups with multiple-layered structures in the core.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The pyrrolic-and graphitic-nitrogen structure of NCQD
ce:section.ce:para: 1HNMR spectra in Fig. 6 provide more evidence for the chemical structure of NCQD. The chemical shift at 11.39 ppm, 6.93 ppm, and 6.62 ppm corresponded to –COOH and Ph-OH protons on the surface of NCQD. These functional groups majorly contribute to the water-soluble properties of NCQD. The peak at 9.86 ppm was associated with Ph-CHO protons. A typical peak at 4.29 ppm–4.50 ppm was assigned to protons in Ar-NH2. The chemical shifts in the regions from 7.62 to 7.64 ppm indicated the presence of protons in –CO–NH-CH2 bonding. The chemical shift at 7.21 ppm was related to Ar–H protons in graphitic structure. The typical peaks at 9.05 ppm–9.63 ppm reveal protons’ existence in graphitic-N structure, while chemical shifts at 8.26 ppm, and 8.63 ppm are attributed to protons in pyrrolic-N structure. Lastly, peaks from 1.13 ppm to 2.61 ppm represent protons in methylene –CH2 moieties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multi-functional surface groups control of the as-prepared NCQD
Confirmation of –OH and NH2 functional groups by FTIR and XPS
ce:section.ce:para: The full XPS spectrum presented in Fig. 7 a showed three peaks at 285.5 eV, 400.3 eV, and 532.8 eV, suggesting that NCQD consisted of C, N, O elements, and the atomic percentages were calculated to 57%, 13%, and 30%, respectively [54]. In Fig. 7b, the high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s were divided into three peaks at 288.6 eV, 286.3 eV, and 285.1 eV, corresponding to -O-CO, C–N/-CN, and C–C/CC, respectively. The N 1s band in Fig. 7c exhibits two typical peaks at 400.1 eV and 401.4 eV, respectively, corresponding to pyrrolic-N (70%) and graphitic-N structures (30%). These two high-resolution bands indicate the successful doping of N into carbon dots. The O 1s band (Fig. 7d) revealed two specific peaks at 532.6 eV and 533.5 eV, attributing to CO/O–CO and C–OH.

The FTIR spectrum was obtained to determine the NCQD's surface functional groups. In Fig. 8 , the FTIR spectra revealed the presence of functional groups including –OH (3527 cm−1), –NH bending in amides (3300 cm−1 and 1602 cm−1), different vibration modes of –CN and C–N (1334 cm−1 and 1286 cm−1, respectively), the stretching of >CO at 1688 cm−1, sp3 C–H stretching (2917 cm−1 and 2846 cm−1), sp2 C–H stretching of aromatic rings (1906 cm−1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determine -NH2 functional groups by fluorescein isothiocyanate (FTIC) assay
ce:section.ce:para: Multi-functional groups on the surface of NCQD play an essential role in defining their applications. Therefore, it is necessary to prove the existence of –NH2, –OH, and –COOH via FTIR, XPS, and NMR. In nanopesticides and theranostic applications, NCQD could function as a fluorescence for bioimaging and as a nanocarrier of active ingredients/drugs. To be an effective and efficient nanocarrier, NCQD should be able to be chemically conjugated with active ingredients/drugs. To prove this concept, Fluorescein-5-isothiocyanate (FTIC), one of the most widely used assays for detecting rich-NH2 compounds, was selected to conjugate with –NH2 on the surface of NCQD.

The principle lies in the reaction between fluorescein-5-isothiocyanate and –NH2 functional groups of NCQD. The reaction is relevant to the central, electrophilic carbon of fluorescein-5-isothiocyanate attacking the nucleophile. As a result, a thiourea conjugation is formed due to the electron shift and proton loss [55]. Fig. 9 depicted the photoluminescence of fluorescein-5-isothiocyanate, NCQD, and fluorescein-5-isothiocyanate-conjugated NCQD. There was a significant decrease in photoluminescence at an emission wavelength of 522 nm [56] and 454 nm [57], compared to the original fluorescein-5-isothiocyanate. In addition, a new peak appeared at 443 nm, confirming the formation of isothiourea conjugation as the products between –NH2 on the surface of NCQD and 5-isothiocyanate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence performance of the as-prepared NCQD
ce:section.ce:para: From Fig. 10 a, the UV–visible absorption spectrum of NCQD showed two peaks at 280 nm and 310 nm, while there was only a typical peak at 280 nm for folic acid [58]. A new strong peak at 310 nm indicated the formation of NCQD due to the decomposition of folic acid from microplasma [59]. Specifically, the absorbance peak at around 280 nm can be attributed to π→π* transitions, revealing the absorbance by aromatic structure (CC bond) of folic acid. Under microplasma treatment, the aromatic system of folic acid was damaged to form NCQD with graphitic nitrogen structure and the introduction of nitrogen and oxygen functional groups at the surface [54,60]. As a result, a new intense absorbance at 310 nm assigned to n→π* transition has appeared in the UV–Vis spectrum of NCQD.

The UV–visible spectra also explained the influence of nitrogen doping on the optical properties of NCQD. Fig. 10b showed that NCQD absorbs the UV–Vis light at 310 nm under excitation of 360 nm to emit an enormously intense blue fluorescence at a wavelength of 454 nm. It was explained that the graphitic nitrogen structure has a lone pair of electrons, which donate to the carbon structure. As a result, NCQD has a positively charged surface, thus increasing the carbon density and reducing the bandgap [54,60].

Quantum yield is an important parameter to evaluate the photoluminescence of NCQD, which is known as the efficiency of converting absorbed light into emitted light in fluorescence [61]. Ideally, a quantum yield of 1.0 means that 100% of absorbed photons are converted to emitted photons. However, compounds with quantum yields above 0.10 are still considered a strong fluorescence in practical terms. In this study, the quantum yield of the as-prepared NCQD was 35%, which is relatively high and good enough for various applications in water purification, nanopesticides, and theranostic treatment. Table 4 compares the quantum yield of NCQD synthesised from various microplasma. The results showed that our current microplasma technology showed a considerable improvement in quantum yield. Most of the microplasma methods produced NCQD with a low quantum yield (less than 10%), while only NCQD in this study and Kurniawan's study are qualified with a quantum yield above 30 [62].

The photoluminescence intensity of NCQD is relatively sensitive to pH values [67]. Thus, it is crucial to study how stable the fluorescence of NCQD is in various pH values, which provides more information for target applications. Deprotonation and protonation are typical mechanisms to explain the pH-dependent fluorescence of NCQD. Remarkably, these mechanisms can change the property and the rate of transition processes, thus affecting the PL spectrum and intensity of the NCQD [68]. Therefore, -COO-, –OH, and –NH2 functional groups on the surface of NCQD may undergo protonation and deprotonation processes [67]. Fig. 11 a showed the difference in photoluminescence against pH values from 2 to 14. The intensity of photoluminescence increased significantly when the pH increased gradually from 2 to 8. However, when the pH value gradually increased from 8 to 14, the photoluminescence intensity decreased significantly. It was explained that the carboxyl group could cause hydrogen bonds intramolecularly and intermolecularly. This result showed the intense vibrational coupling of the OH functional groups in the surface state of NCQD, thus which induce the broadness of energy level and bands of the absorption spectra of NCQD at a low pH value.

Once increasing the pH values from 2 to 8, the carboxyl group's proton dissociation process was more dominant, thus decreasing the effect of intramolecular and intermolecular hydrogen bonds significantly. As a result, there was an increase in photoluminescence intensity without changing the photoluminescence spectrum. In contrast, the hydroxyl group interacted with emission sites, and a new surface state in NCQD with a lower bandgap was formed due to its deprotonation process. When the pH values increase from 8 to 14, the carboxyl groups become deprotonated. On the other hand, the hydroxyl groups undergo a slow deprotonation process, which leads to a decrease in photoluminescence intensity.

Solvents are also one of the most critical parameters in the multi-functional applications of the as-prepared NCQD [69]. In this study, NCQD were dissolved into hydrophilic (water and ethanol) and hydrophobic solvents (acetone and acetonitrile). Fig. 11b indicated that NCQD dissolved well in water or ethanol to perform the intense blue photoluminescence. In contrast, hydrophobic solvents (acetone and acetonitrile) showed low solubility performance with weak blue photoluminescence. In water or ethanol, NCQD undergoes the swelling and the changing of proton donor-acceptor properties in the carbon core, which changes their conformation. Thus, NCQD can express excitation wavelength-dependent behaviour. More specifically, in polar solvents such as water or ethanol, pyrrolic- and graphitic-nitrogen in the core of NCQD can enhance the charge-carrier density by interacting with the surrounding molecules in solvents.

Additionally, by changing the dipole direction of the surrounding solvent molecules, excited electrons from the ground state can be stabilised. It is required more energy to change the dipole direction of the solvent molecules when increasing the polarity of solvents. For this reason, exciting electrons in a high polar solvent (water or ethanol) lose more energy to emit a strong blue fluorescence at 454 nm. Otherwise, low polar solvents like acetone and acetonitrile did not exhibit intense blue fluorescence at the emission wavelength of 454 nm.
Overall, the as-prepared NCQD has shown the potential in various applications) water treatment, hydrophilic pesticides and theranostic medicine) with the strongest blue photoluminescence in water.

The DLVO theory is a model to describe the behaviour of colloidal dispersion [70]. In this theory, the zeta potential and particle size are used to interpret how two nanoparticles interact in an aqueous solution [71,72]. According to DLVO, the zeta potential of nanoparticles is the key to the stability of nanofluids. From this principle, the DLVO model can predict the optimal conditions of zeta potential to obtain the most stable NCQD in an aqueous solution. Therefore, the motivation for using the DLVO model in this study is to understand the colloidal behaviours in an aqueous solution with various zeta potentials to create stable NCQD. This study showed four scenarios with different zeta potentials in Table 2 and Fig. 12 . The DLVO models of NCQD dispersed in water are plotted with a mean particle size of 3.13 nm at 298.15 0K, and the distance between two NCQD was hypothesised from 0 to 10 nm. VD and VH represent dispersion and attraction forces between two NCQD. Therefore, the colloidal behaviours of NCQD rely on the total energy of VD and VH, known as VT.

When increasing the zeta-potential to ±10 and ± 20 mV in scenarios 2 (Figs. 12b) and 3 (Fig. 12c), electrical double layer interactions of NCQD in water increase significantly. This result explained that the number of functional groups (-NH2, –COOH, and –OH) on the surface of NCQD in these two models increased significantly, leading to enhanced electrical double layer interactions. However, the total energy curve of NCQD in water still shows a negative curve, meaning that at this condition, NCQD were still not stable in water, giving the chances of aggregation.

Compared with scenarios 1, 2, and 3, the DLVO model in scenario 4 (Fig. 12d) depicted the most stable NCQD with zeta potential ±40 mV. At this condition, the electrical double layer interactions of NCQD were by far higher than in the other three scenarios, resulting in the total energy of the curve being positive. This result means that the as-prepared NCQD are stable in the form of suspension with a distance of 0.6 nm. Finally, the prediction from DLVO theory provided vital information that increasing the zeta potential value may lead to more stable NCQD, which can be used in the targeting applications (nanopesticides, water purification, and theranostics).

N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQD) are rationally designed and synthesised by the kINPen®IND microplasma jet with properties required for three main applications, including nanopesticides, water purification, and theranostics. The results indicate that three molecular targets guiding the rational designs have been achieved in a proof of principle fashion. The NCQD were monodispersed with a mean particle size of 3.13 nm. The core structure of NCQD was in the form of a crystalline. It consisted of multilayered graphitic- and pyrrolic-nitrogen defected graphene sheets, while their surface was decorated by –NH2, –OH, and –COOH functional groups. The resulting NCQD had an intensely blue fluorescence performance with a QY of 35%, which was the highest among other plasma synthesis approaches and the best of all methods applied.

Regarding fluorescence stability, NCQD showed the most intense fluorescence in the range of pH values of 6–8. By performing the strongest fluorescence in water, the NCQD was considered hydrophilic, which is in line with the presence of protic and polar surface groups. DLVO theory predicts that at a potential of ±40 mV, NCQD may have the most stable colloidal behaviour in water.

As said and being critical, we have explored many steps, and this study was proof of concept. This already has advanced literature and considerably in some relevant properties. The outcome of this study was to provide a valuable contribution to this field, introducing a new synthetic approach with microplasma for NCQD with appropriate properties. Yet, the ambition was to molecularly tailor nanoparticles in the sense of (1) having only one functional group on the surface instead of several, at a defined and controllable surface density, (2) having only one bulk-constituting material with definition on the molecular level, and (3) excluding undesired atomic inclusions such as oxygen where possible. Finally, we aim to make nanomaterial products distinct from being a ‘performance chemical’ for an envisaged application.

This study is a part of our research series on the N-doped carbon quantum dots. Our first literature review has pointed out the potential applications of this material in agriculture as nanopesticides [34]. At the same time, some literature also proved their applicability in water purification [36] and theranostic treatment [41]. Due to its outstanding properties, which are applicable for various applications, it is a driving force for us to survey and comprehensively assess NCQD synthesis processes using green chemistry and circular and Ecoscale metrics [47]. This survey indicates that microplasma is one of the most sustainable methods in terms of energy efficiency while lacking mass efficiency. Another study has been carried out to improve the mass efficiency of NCQDs by a process intensification to obtain gram-scale production. Future work is underway to characterise the efficiency of our rationally designed NCQD for applications as nanopesticides, water purification, and theranostic treatment.

After demonstrating the potential applications of the as-prepared NCQD, industrial-scale production is another challenge in bringing this outstanding NCQD to the market. Microplasma jet has lower power generation than other plasma reactors, which might result in low mass efficiency. However, the microplasma jet is way more advanced than other plasma reactors in terms of safety, process efficiency, simple operation, economical, and high-quality product. The authors believe that mass production efficiency is significant in industrial production. Still, other factors such as safety, process efficiency, simple operation, economics, and high-quality product should be highly considered in terms of sustainable production, which will define the total cost of industrial production. Especially in biomedical applications, an excellent fine product is required.

Several ideas have been proposed to mitigate large-scale production issues: (1) an array of microplasma jets can be developed to up-scale the production of high-quality NCQD in an energy-efficient, eco-friendly, and safe manner while still ensuring high mass efficiency; (2) another device that could be used for further study is PlasmaLeap, where bubble microplasma is formed. It is believed that these two proposed approaches can enhance the large-scale production of NCQD.

Quoc Hue Pho: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Data curation, Writing – original draft. LiangLiang Lin: Writing – review & editing. Nam Nghiep Tran: Writing – review & editing. Tung T.
